## Goal
Test the complete user workflow from backup through activation and switching.

## Workflow Steps

### 1. Initial Setup
- Create mock auth files for multiple providers
- Initialize vault

### 2. Backup Phase
```
backup codex account1@example.com
backup codex account2@example.com
backup claude personal@example.com
```

### 3. Activation Phase
```
activate codex account1@example.com
verify auth files match account1
activate codex account2@example.com
verify auth files match account2
```

### 4. Switch Phase
```
status codex  # shows account2
activate codex account1@example.com
status codex  # shows account1
```

### 5. Auto-Backup Verification
- Verify _original profile created on first activate
- Verify auto-backup timestamps

## Test File
internal/e2e/workflows/backup_activate_test.go

## Logging Requirements
- Log each step with timestamps
- Log auth file contents (redacted)
- Log vault state after each operation
- Duration metrics for each operation

- Vault state consistent

 {"id":"caam-fu2w","title":"EPIC: Live Usage Monitor & Alerts","description":"# EPIC: Live Usage Monitor

## Strategic Context

When managing multiple AI accounts, users want real-time visibility into the status of all accounts. This is different from the TUI (which focuses on profile management) - this is a MONITORING dashboard that shows live rate limit status, refresh continuously, and alerts when any account needs attention.

## Use Cases

1. **Background Monitoring**: Keep a terminal window showing all account status
2. **tmux/Screen Integration**: Show status in status bar
3. **Alert Notifications**: Desktop notifications when limits approaching
4. **Scripting Integration**: Machine-readable status for custom workflows

## User Experience

### Interactive Monitor
```
$ caam monitor

╔═══════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════╗
║                    LIVE USAGE MONITOR                                  ║
║                    Last updated: 12:34:56                              ║
╠═══════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════╣
║                                                                         ║
║  CLAUDE                                                                 ║
║  ────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────  ║
║  alice@gmail.com  🟢 ████████░░░░░░░░░░░░ 42% │ resets 4h 12m │ ready  ║
║  bob@gmail.com    🟡 ██████████████░░░░░░ 71% │ resets 1h 45m │ ready  ║
║  carol@gmail.com  🔴 ████████████████████ 95% │ resets 0h 23m │ cooldown║
║                                                                         ║
║  CODEX                                                                  ║
║  ────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────  ║
║  work@company.com 🟢 ████░░░░░░░░░░░░░░░░ 18% │ resets 3h 56m │ ready  ║
║                                                                         ║
║  GEMINI                                                                 ║
║  ────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────  ║
║  (no profiles configured)                                               ║
║                                                                         ║
╚═══════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════╝
  Refresh: 30s │ [r] refresh now │ [p] precheck │ [q] quit
```

### tmux Integration
```bash
# In .tmux.conf
set -g status-right '#(caam monitor --format brief)'

# Shows: claude:42%▲ codex:18%▲
```

### Alert Mode
```
$ caam monitor --alert --threshold 80

[2024-01-10 12:34:56] ⚠️  claude/carol@gmail.com at 85%
[2024-01-10 12:35:12] 🔴 claude/carol@gmail.com at 90%
[2024-01-10 12:35:45] 🔴 claude/carol@gmail.com at 95% - SWITCH RECOMMENDED
```

## Implementation

### Monitor State
```go
// internal/monitor/monitor.go

type Monitor struct {
    interval   time.Duration
    providers  []string
    
    fetcher    *usage.MultiProfileFetcher
    vault      *authfile.Vault
    healthStore *health.Storage
    db         *db.DB
    
    state      *MonitorState
    mu         sync.RWMutex
}

type MonitorState struct {
    Profiles  map[string]*ProfileState  // "provider/name" -> state
    UpdatedAt time.Time
    Errors    []string
}

type ProfileState struct {
    Provider     string
    ProfileName  string
    Usage        *usage.UsageInfo
    Health       health.HealthStatus
    PoolStatus   authpool.PoolStatus
    InCooldown   bool
    CooldownUntil *time.Time
    Alert        *Alert
}

type Alert struct {
    Type    AlertType
    Message string
    Since   time.Time
}
```

### Output Renderers
```go
// internal/monitor/render.go

type Renderer interface {
    Render(state *MonitorState) string
}

type TableRenderer struct {}    // Rich ASCII table
type BriefRenderer struct {}    // One-line summary
type JSONRenderer struct {}     // Machine-readable
type AlertRenderer struct {}    // Alerts only
```

### Alert System
```go
// internal/monitor/alerts.go

type AlertManager struct {
    threshold   float64
    notifier    Notifier
    history     []*Alert
}

type Notifier interface {
    Notify(alert *Alert) error
}

type DesktopNotifier struct {}  // libnotify / osascript
type TerminalNotifier struct {} // Print to stderr
type WebhookNotifier struct {}  // POST to URL
```

### Background Polling
```go
func (m *Monitor) Start(ctx context.Context) error {
    ticker := time.NewTicker(m.interval)
    defer ticker.Stop()
    
    for {
        select {
        case <-ctx.Done():
            return nil
        case <-ticker.C:
            m.refresh(ctx)
        }
    }
}

func (m *Monitor) refresh(ctx context.Context) {
    // 1. List all profiles from vault
    // 2. Fetch usage for each (concurrent)
    // 3. Check health status
    // 4. Check cooldown status
    // 5. Generate alerts
    // 6. Update state
}
```

## Commands

### caam monitor
```
Usage:
  caam monitor [flags]

Flags:
  -i, --interval duration   Refresh interval (default 30s)
  -p, --provider strings    Providers to monitor (default: all)
  -f, --format string       Output format: table, brief, json, alerts
  -a, --alert               Enable alert mode (log alerts to stderr)
  -t, --threshold float     Alert threshold (default 0.8)
  -n, --notify              Enable desktop notifications
  -w, --webhook string      Webhook URL for alerts
  -1, --once                Fetch once and exit (no live monitoring)
```

### Examples
```bash
# Interactive monitor
caam monitor

# tmux status bar integration
caam monitor --format brief --once

# Alert mode with desktop notifications
caam monitor --alert --notify --threshold 80

# JSON output for scripting
caam monitor --format json --once | jq '.profiles[] | select(.usage.primary_percent > 80)'

# Monitor specific provider
caam monitor --provider claude
```

## Integration Points

### TUI Integration
Add monitor panel to existing TUI (internal/tui/):
- Real-time usage bars for each profile
- Alert indicators
- Quick switch actions

### Daemon Integration  
Monitor can run as part of daemon for:
- Background alert generation
- Desktop notifications
- Webhook triggers

## Deliverables
1. Monitor struct and state management
2. Background polling loop
3. Table renderer (rich ASCII)
4. Brief renderer (one-line)
5. JSON renderer
6. Alert system with thresholds
7. Desktop notification integration
8. Webhook notification
9. caam monitor command
10. TUI integration (optional)

## Dependencies
- internal/usage/ (for fetching rate limits)
- internal/health/ (for health status)
- internal/db/ (for cooldown status)
- EPIC: Background Auth Pool Daemon (for pool status)

## Enables
- Real-time visibility into account status
- Proactive notifications before limits hit
- tmux/screen integration for always-visible status

 {"id":"caam-fwz","title":"OAuth refresh handler for Claude Code","description":"# OAuth Refresh Handler for Claude Code

## Purpose
Implement OAuth token refresh for Claude Code to automatically renew tokens before expiry.

## Research Required
**IMPORTANT**: Claude Code OAuth implementation needs reverse engineering.

### Questions to Answer
1. Where is the oauth.json file located?
2. What is the token endpoint URL?
3. What is the client_id?
4. Is it JSON body or form-encoded?
5. What scopes are required?

### Likely Locations to Check
- `~/.claude/oauth.json`
- `~/.claude.ai/oauth.json`  
- `~/.config/claude/oauth.json`
- Check Claude Code source/docs if available

## Technical Requirements (Template)

### Refresh Function
```go
func RefreshClaudeToken(ctx context.Context, refreshToken string) (*TokenResponse, error) {
    // Build request
    body := map[string]string{
        "client_id":     claudeClientID,
        "grant_type":    "refresh_token",
        "refresh_token": refreshToken,
        // "scope":      "...",  // if needed
    }
    
    req, err := http.NewRequestWithContext(ctx, "POST", claudeTokenURL, ...)
    req.Header.Set("Content-Type", "application/json")  // or form-encoded
    
    // Execute
    resp, err := http.DefaultClient.Do(req)
    if err != nil {
        return nil, fmt.Errorf("refresh request failed: %w", err)
    }
    defer resp.Body.Close()
    
    // Parse response
    if resp.StatusCode != 200 {
        // Handle error
    }
    
    var tokenResp TokenResponse
    json.NewDecoder(resp.Body).Decode(&tokenResp)
    
    return &tokenResp, nil
}
```

### TokenResponse Structure
```go
type TokenResponse struct {
    AccessToken  string `json:"access_token"`
    RefreshToken string `json:"refresh_token,omitempty"` // May rotate
    ExpiresIn    int    `json:"expires_in"`              // Seconds
    ExpiresAt    string `json:"expires_at,omitempty"`    // ISO8601
    TokenType    string `json:"token_type"`
}
```

### File Update
```go
func UpdateClaudeAuth(path string, resp *TokenResponse) error {
    // Read existing file
    existing, _ := os.ReadFile(path)
    var auth map[string]any
    json.Unmarshal(existing, &auth)
    
    // Update fields
    auth["access_token"] = resp.AccessToken
    if resp.RefreshToken != "" {
        auth["refresh_token"] = resp.RefreshToken
    }
    auth["expires_at"] = resp.ExpiresAt
    // ... or calculate from expires_in
    
    // Atomic write
    return atomicWriteJSON(path, auth)
}
```

## Safety Requirements
1. **Never lose refresh token** - if refresh fails, keep original
2. **Atomic writes** - use temp file + rename
3. **Backup original** - keep .bak file before overwrite
4. **Verify new token** - optionally test before committing

## Error Handling
- Invalid refresh token → mark profile as "needs login"
- Network error → retry with backoff
- Rate limited → respect Retry-After header
- Unknown error → log and keep original token

## Acceptance Criteria
- [ ] Research: Document Claude Code OAuth endpoints
- [ ] RefreshClaudeToken function implemented
- [ ] UpdateClaudeAuth with atomic writes
- [ ] Unit tests with mock HTTP server
- [ ] Integration test with real credentials (optional, manual)
- [ ] Error handling for all failure modes

- `internal/refresh/claude.go` (new)
- `internal/refresh/claude_test.go` (new)

- Token expiry parsing (for reading current state)","status":"closed","priority":1,"issue_type":"task","created_at":"2025-12-17T16:20:23.871008574-05:00","updated_at":"2025-12-17T17:35:02.534599402-05:00","closed_at":"2025-12-17T17:35:02.534599402-05:00","close_reason":"Implemented OAuth refresh logic for Claude Code. ## Overview

## Technical Details

### Aggregation Functions

System notifications for token expiry, rate limits, and background operations.

## ⚠️ Priority Note: P3 (Backlog)
Desktop notifications add significant complexity:
- External dependency (beeep or similar library)
- Cross-platform testing (macOS, Linux, Windows)
- Daemon integration
- Notification throttling logic
- User preference management

Consider this a polish feature after core functionality is solid.

## Problem Statement
The daemon runs in background doing token refresh and health checks, but has no way to alert users when something needs attention.

## Alternative Approaches Worth Considering

### Alternative 1: CLI Warning on Next Run
Instead of push notifications, show warnings when user runs any caam command:
```
$ caam ls
⚠️ Warning: Claude profile 'work' token expires in 2 hours
   Run: caam refresh claude work
```
This is simpler and doesn't require external dependencies.

### Alternative 2: Status File
Write a status file the user's shell prompt can check:
```
~/.local/share/caam/status.json
```
Shell integration can show alerts in prompt. Already have shell integration from caam-k9z.

### Alternative 3: True Desktop Notifications
Full beeep integration as originally planned.

## If Implementing Full Notifications

### Design Decisions
- Use `beeep` library for cross-platform support
- Config controls notification levels:
```yaml
notifications:
  enabled: true
  levels:
    critical: true    # Token expired, auth failure
    warning: true     # Token expiring soon, high penalty
    info: false       # Sync complete, backup complete
```
- Rate limit notifications (max 1 per minute per type)
- Respect system DND/Focus modes where possible

### Implementation Tasks
1. Add notifications package using beeep
2. Add NotificationConfig to config package
3. Integrate with daemon for token expiry checks
4. Integrate with wrap for rate limit events
5. Add `caam notify test` command
6. Add notification throttling

## Recommendation
Start with Alternative 1 (CLI warnings) which provides most value with minimal complexity. Only pursue full desktop notifications if users explicitly request it.

 {"id":"caam-k9z","title":"Shell integration: prompt, completion, auto-activate","description":"## Overview
Deep shell integration that makes caam invisible during daily use. Users type familiar commands (claude, codex) and caam handles everything behind the scenes.

## The Problem
Users must consciously use caam commands:
```bash
caam activate claude work
claude "help me"
# ... later ...
caam next claude
claude "continue"
```

This requires:
- Remembering to use caam
- Knowing current profile state
- Manual switching

## The Solution
Shell integration with three components:

### 1. Smart Aliases (caam shell-init)
```bash
# User adds to .bashrc/.zshrc:
eval "$(caam shell-init)"

# This creates functions that wrap the AI CLIs:
# claude() { caam run claude "$@"; }
# codex() { caam run codex "$@"; }
# gemini() { caam run gemini "$@"; }
```

Now `claude "prompt"` automatically:
- Uses the best available profile
- Handles rate limits transparently
- Records usage for analytics

### 2. Tab Completion
```bash
caam activate cl<TAB>     → claude
caam activate claude w<TAB> → work-account-1
caam switch <TAB>         → [shows all profiles with health]
```

Uses Cobra's built-in completion + dynamic profile completion.

### 3. Prompt Integration (Optional)
Show current profile in shell prompt:
```bash
# In PS1/PROMPT:
alice@work ~/code (claude:work-1) $

# Or as right-prompt showing all:
                                [claude:work codex:main]
```

### 4. Auto-Activation (direnv-style)
```bash
cd ~/work/backend
# caam: Activated workspace 'work' for this directory
#       claude: work-account
#       codex: work-codex

cd ~/personal/side-project  
# caam: Activated workspace 'personal'
#       claude: personal
```

## Technical Design

### Shell Init Command
`caam shell-init [bash|zsh|fish]` outputs shell-specific code:

**Bash/Zsh:**
```bash
# Wrapper functions
claude() { caam run claude "$@"; }
codex() { caam run codex "$@"; }
gemini() { caam run gemini "$@"; }

# Completion
eval "$(caam completion bash)"

# Prompt helper (optional)
_caam_prompt() {
  caam status --format=prompt 2>/dev/null
}

# Auto-activation hook (optional)
_caam_chpwd() {
  caam project check --quiet
}
chpwd_functions+=(_caam_chpwd)  # zsh
# or PROMPT_COMMAND for bash
```

**Fish:**
```fish
function claude; caam run claude $argv; end
function codex; caam run codex $argv; end
# etc.
```

### Dynamic Completion
Profile names must be completed dynamically:
```go
// In cmd/caam/cmd/activate.go
ValidArgsFunction: func(cmd *cobra.Command, args []string, toComplete string) ([]string, cobra.ShellCompDirective) {
    if len(args) == 0 {
        // Complete tool names
        return []string{"claude", "codex", "gemini"}, cobra.ShellCompDirectiveNoFileComp
    }
    // Complete profile names for the given tool
    profiles := vault.List(args[0])
    return profiles, cobra.ShellCompDirectiveNoFileComp
}
```

### Prompt Status
`caam status --format=prompt` returns minimal string:
```
claude:work codex:main
# or with health indicators:
claude:work(🟢) codex:main(🟡)
```

### Auto-Activation Hook
When directory changes:
1. Check if current dir (or parents) have `.caam/` config
2. If workspace defined, activate it
3. If profile mappings defined, activate per-tool
4. Cache result to avoid repeated lookups

## Configuration
```yaml
shell:
  auto_aliases: true           # Create wrapper functions
  completion: true             # Enable tab completion
  prompt_integration: false    # Show in prompt (opt-in)
  auto_activate: true          # direnv-style activation
  
  # Prompt format
  prompt_format: "caam:%t:%p"  # %t=tool, %p=profile
  prompt_show_health: true     # Include health indicator
```

## User Experience

### Installation
```bash
$ caam shell-init --install
Adding to ~/.zshrc:
  eval "$(caam shell-init zsh)"

Restart your shell or run:
  source ~/.zshrc

Done! Now 'claude', 'codex', 'gemini' will use caam automatically.
```

### Daily Use
```bash
$ claude "fix this bug"
# Just works - caam picks best profile, handles limits

$ cd ~/work/backend
# caam: Using workspace 'work'

$ claude "continue the refactor"
# Uses work profile automatically
```

### Completion Demo
```bash
$ caam activate <TAB>
claude    codex    gemini

$ caam activate claude <TAB>
work-1         (🟢 active, expires 23h)
work-2         (🟡 expires 2h)
personal       (🔴 in cooldown)
```

## Implementation Tasks
1. **Shell init command** (cmd/caam/cmd/shell_init.go)
   - Generate shell-specific init code
   - Support bash, zsh, fish
   - --install flag for auto-add to rc file

2. **Dynamic completion** (cmd/caam/cmd/completion.go)
   - Profile name completion
   - Health indicators in completion descriptions
   - Tool name completion

3. **Prompt helper** (cmd/caam/cmd/status.go)
   - --format=prompt flag
   - Minimal output for PS1

4. **Auto-activation hook** (cmd/caam/cmd/project.go)
   - project check --quiet command
   - Workspace activation logic

## Success Criteria
- [ ] `eval "$(caam shell-init)"` works for bash/zsh/fish
- [ ] Tab completion works for all commands
- [ ] Profile names complete with health hints
- [ ] Prompt integration is optional but works
- [ ] Auto-activation triggers on cd (zsh chpwd, bash PROMPT_COMMAND)
- [ ] Performance: shell init adds <10ms to shell startup

## Dependencies
- Cobra completion (built-in)
- Wrapper mode (caam-q9f) for the aliases to work

 {"id":"caam-m5a","title":"Display QR code for device auth URL (nice-to-have)","description":"# Display QR Code for Device Auth URL

## What

When using device code flow, display a QR code that users can scan with their phone to quickly reach the authorization URL.

## Why

QR codes provide faster mobile access:
- No typing long URLs on phones
- Works even when URL can't be copy-pasted (some SSH clients)
- Professional/polished UX
- Many CLI tools (gh, az) do this successfully

## Implementation

### Dependencies

Use a pure-Go QR code library to avoid external dependencies:
- github.com/mdp/qrterminal (MIT license, popular)
- github.com/skip2/go-qrcode (generates image, not terminal)

### File: internal/auth/qrcode.go

\`\`\`go
package auth

import (
    "io"
    "os"
    
    "github.com/mdp/qrterminal/v3"
    "golang.org/x/term"
)

// DisplayQRCode prints a QR code to the terminal if stdout is a TTY
func DisplayQRCode(url string, w io.Writer) {
    // Only show QR if we have a TTY (not piped/redirected)
    if !term.IsTerminal(int(os.Stdout.Fd())) {
        return
    }
    
    // Use ASCII mode for compatibility
    config := qrterminal.Config{
        Level:     qrterminal.L,
        Writer:    w,
        BlackChar: qrterminal.WHITE, // Inverted for dark terminals
        WhiteChar: qrterminal.BLACK,
        QuietZone: 1,
    }
    qrterminal.GenerateWithConfig(url, config)
}

func (p *Provider) loginWithDeviceCode(ctx context.Context, prof *profile.Profile) error {
    fmt.Println("Starting device code authentication...")

 {"id":"caam-m9g","title":"EPIC: Just Works UX - Zero-friction account switching","description":"## Vision
Transform CAAM from "useful CLI tool" to "invisible magic that just works."

## Success Criteria
- New user productive in <2 minutes (vs current 10+ minutes)
- Daily use requires zero caam commands (shell wrapper handles everything)
- Rate limit recovery is automatic (detect, cooldown, switch, retry)
- Profile management is intuitive (aliases, workspaces, fuzzy matching)

## Core Features (Priority Order)

### P0 - Foundation (Must complete first)
- [x] **caam-q9f**: Wrapper Mode - `caam run <tool>` with auto rate-limit detection and retry
- [x] **caam-bxq**: Init Wizard - Guided setup with auth discovery

### P1 - Shell Integration (Enables invisible UX)
- [ ] **caam-k9z**: Shell integration - aliases, completion, prompt, auto-activate
- [ ] **caam-hxk**: `caam next` - One-command profile rotation

### P2 - Power Features
- [ ] **caam-08w**: Profile aliases and fuzzy matching
- [ ] **caam-t5p**: Profile workspaces - Switch all tools at once
- [ ] **caam-lr2**: `caam add` - One-command account capture
- [ ] **caam-ah4**: Auth file watcher with auto-backup

### P3 - Polish
- [ ] **caam-vog**: `caam verify` - Validate all profile tokens
- [ ] **caam-0l0**: Background daemon for auto-refresh and sync

## Dependency Graph
```
                    ┌─────────────────┐
                    │   caam-m9g      │
                    │  (this epic)    │
                    └────────┬────────┘
                             │
        ┌────────────────────┼────────────────────┐
        │                    │                    │
        ▼                    ▼                    ▼
   ┌─────────┐         ┌─────────┐         ┌─────────┐
   │caam-q9f │         │caam-bxq │         │caam-hxk │
   │ wrapper │         │  init   │         │  next   │
   └────┬────┘         └─────────┘         └─────────┘
        │
        ▼
   ┌─────────┐
   │caam-k9z │ (shell integration uses wrapper)
   │  shell  │
   └─────────┘
```

## Design Principles
1. **Invisible by default** - Works without thinking about it
2. **Progressive disclosure** - Simple commands, advanced options available
3. **Safe by default** - Auto-backup, no data loss
4. **Fast feedback** - Clear status, quick operations

## Anti-patterns to Avoid
- Requiring users to understand internal architecture
- Multi-step workflows for common operations
- Silent failures or unclear error messages
- Configuration complexity

## What "Just Works" Means

**Before (current state):**
```bash
# User hits rate limit in Claude
# Manually notices the error
caam cooldown set claude/work
caam ls claude  # remember what profiles exist
caam activate claude personal
claude "retry my prompt"
```

**After (with this epic complete):**
```bash
claude "do something"
# Rate limit happens automatically handled
# User sees: "⚠️ Rate limit. Switching to 'personal'..."
# Command continues with new profile
```

## Implementation Order
1. **caam-q9f** (Wrapper Mode) - Core capability
2. **caam-bxq** (Init Wizard) - Onboarding experience
3. **caam-k9z** (Shell Integration) - Makes wrapper invisible
4. **caam-hxk** (caam next) - Quick manual switching
5. P2/P3 features in any order

## Measuring Success
- Time to first profile saved: target <2 minutes
- Daily caam commands required: target 0 (shell wrapper handles all)
- Rate limit recovery time: target <5 seconds (automatic)
- User confusion incidents: target 0 (intuitive UX)","status":"closed","priority":1,"issue_type":"epic","created_at":"2025-12-19T19:02:55.725871327-05:00","updated_at":"2025-12-19T21:50:19.217733098-05:00","closed_at":"2025-12-19T21:50:19.217733098-05:00","close_reason":"All sub-items complete: P0 (wrapper, init), P1 (shell, next), P2 (aliases, workspaces, add, authwatch), P3 (verify, daemon).  {"id":"caam-ogpo","title":"Performance benchmarks for critical sub-100ms paths","description":"# Task: Performance benchmarks for critical sub-100ms paths

## Overview
Benchmark tests to ensure critical operations meet the sub-100ms latency target that makes caam feel instant.

## Why This Matters
caam differentiates by being FAST:
- Profile switching: <50ms (not 30 seconds with browser)
- Auth file swap: <10ms
- Rate limit detection: <1ms per line
- PTY injection: <5ms

If these are slow, caam loses its value proposition.

## Benchmarks

### 1. Auth File Swap
```go
func BenchmarkAuthFileSwap(b *testing.B) {
    vault := authfile.NewTestVault(b)
    vault.CreateProfile("claude", "alice", largeAuthData)
    vault.CreateProfile("claude", "bob", largeAuthData)
    
    b.ResetTimer()
    for i := 0; i < b.N; i++ {
        profile := []string{"alice", "bob"}[i%2]
        vault.SwapActive(profile)
    }
    
    // Target: <10ms per swap
    // Report: b.ReportMetric(float64(elapsed)/float64(b.N), "ns/swap")
}
```

### 2. Rate Limit Detection
```go
func BenchmarkRateLimitDetection(b *testing.B) {
    detector := ratelimit.NewDetector(ratelimit.DefaultPatterns())
    lines := loadTestLines(1000) // Mix of normal and rate limit lines
    
    b.ResetTimer()
    for i := 0; i < b.N; i++ {
        for _, line := range lines {
            detector.Check(line)
        }
    }
    
    // Target: <1ms per 1000 lines
}
```

### 3. PTY Command Injection
```go
func BenchmarkPTYInjection(b *testing.B) {
    ctrl := pty.NewTestController(b)
    
    b.ResetTimer()
    for i := 0; i < b.N; i++ {
        ctrl.InjectCommand("/login")
    }
    
    // Target: <5ms per injection
}
```

### 4. JWT Parsing
```go
func BenchmarkJWTParsing(b *testing.B) {
    token := loadTestJWT() // Large JWT with many claims
    
    b.ResetTimer()
    for i := 0; i < b.N; i++ {
        identity.ExtractFromJWT(token)
    }
    
    // Target: <1ms per parse
}
```

### 5. Log Parsing (per file)
```go
func BenchmarkLogParsing(b *testing.B) {
    logFile := createTestLogFile(10000) // 10k entries
    scanner := logs.NewClaudeScanner()
    
    b.ResetTimer()
    for i := 0; i < b.N; i++ {
        scanner.ScanFile(ctx, logFile, time.Time{})
    }
    
    // Target: <100ms per 10k entries
}
```

### 6. Burn Rate Calculation
```go
func BenchmarkBurnRateCalc(b *testing.B) {
    entries := generateTestEntries(1000)
    
    b.ResetTimer()
    for i := 0; i < b.N; i++ {
        usage.CalculateBurnRate(entries, 2*time.Hour)
    }
    
    // Target: <10ms per calculation
}
```

### 7. Full Handoff Latency (E2E)
```go
func BenchmarkHandoffLatency(b *testing.B) {
    runner := setupTestRunner(b)
    
    b.ResetTimer()
    for i := 0; i < b.N; i++ {
        start := time.Now()
        runner.SimulateRateLimitAndHandoff()
        elapsed := time.Since(start)
        
        if elapsed > 5*time.Second {
            b.Errorf("Handoff too slow: %v", elapsed)
        }
    }
    
    // Target: <5 seconds total (including login wait)
}
```

## Performance Targets Summary
| Operation | Target | Critical |
|-----------|--------|----------|
| Auth file swap | <10ms | Yes |
| Rate limit check (per line) | <0.1ms | Yes |
| PTY injection | <5ms | Yes |
| JWT parsing | <1ms | No |
| Log file parse (10k lines) | <100ms | No |
| Burn rate calculation | <10ms | No |
| Full handoff (E2E) | <5s | Yes |

## Reporting
- Run benchmarks in CI
- Track regression over time
- Alert if critical paths exceed targets
- Generate flamegraphs for slow operations

## Acceptance Criteria
- [ ] All critical paths benchmarked
- [ ] Tests pass on CI
- [ ] Results logged with clear metrics
- [ ] No critical path exceeds target
- [ ] Flamegraph generation for profiling

    t.Logf("[TEST] Creation result: err=%v", err)

    // Write test input

    t.Logf("[TEST] Read output: %q, err=%v", output, err)
    
    require.Contains(t, output, "hello")
    
    t.Log("[TEST] Closing PTY")
    ctrl.Close()
}

### 3. Pattern Waiting Tests
```go
func TestWaitForPattern(t *testing.T) {
    tests := []struct {
        name     string
        pattern  string
        output   string
        timeout  time.Duration
        wantErr  bool
    }{
        {
            name:    "pattern found",
            pattern: "SUCCESS",
            output:  "Processing...\nSUCCESS: done",
            timeout: time.Second,
            wantErr: false,
        },
        {
            name:    "pattern not found - timeout",
            pattern: "SUCCESS",
            output:  "ERROR: failed",
            timeout: 100 * time.Millisecond,
            wantErr: true,
        },
    }
    
    for _, tc := range tests {
        t.Run(tc.name, func(t *testing.T) {
            t.Logf("[TEST] Pattern: %q", tc.pattern)
            t.Logf("[TEST] Expected output: %q", tc.output)
            t.Logf("[TEST] Timeout: %v", tc.timeout)
            
            // ... test execution ...
            
            t.Logf("[TEST] Result: err=%v, wantErr=%v", err, tc.wantErr)
        })
    }
}
```

func TestPlatformSupport(t *testing.T) {

    switch runtime.GOOS {
    case "linux", "darwin":
        t.Log("[TEST] Unix PTY support expected")

    }
}

## Data Sources

The precheck command aggregates:
1. Real-time rate limits (internal/usage/)
2. Token consumption from logs (EPIC: Log File Parsing)
3. Identity info (EPIC: Identity & JWT Extraction)
4. Burn rate calculations (EPIC: Enhanced Rate Limit Tracking)
5. Cooldown status (internal/db/)
6. Health scores (internal/health/)
7. Auth pool status (EPIC: Background Auth Pool Daemon)

## Implementation

### PrecheckReport Struct
```go
type PrecheckReport struct {
    Provider        string
    Timestamp       time.Time
    
    Recommended     *ProfileReport
    Backups         []*ProfileReport
    InCooldown      []*ProfileReport
    
    TodayConsumption *ConsumptionSummary
    Forecast        *SessionForecast
    
    RecommendedCommand string
}

type ProfileReport struct {
    ProfileName     string
    Identity        *identity.Identity
    RateLimits      *usage.UsageInfo
    BurnRate        *usage.BurnRateInfo
    HealthStatus    health.HealthStatus
    PoolStatus      authpool.PoolStatus
    CooldownUntil   *time.Time
    EstHeadroom     time.Duration
    SuggestedAction string
}

type ConsumptionSummary struct {
    TotalTokens     int64
    AvgBurnRate     float64
    PeakBurnRate    float64
    TokensByModel   map[string]int64
}

type SessionForecast struct {
    TotalHeadroom   time.Duration
    RotationPlan    []*RotationStep
    Warning         string
}

type RotationStep struct {
    ProfileName     string
    StartAt         time.Time
    EstDuration     time.Duration
    Reason          string
}
```
